serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of pesto pasta is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 460 kcal, 25 g fat, 41 g carbohydrate, and 15 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Pesto Pasta — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pesto Pasta (cr_id F05060095). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (white, boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 49 is consistent with published values for white pasta (boiled, al dente) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). Pesto sauce does not materially alter the GI of the pasta base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 41.2) / 100 ≈ 20.2 for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 329 mg/100 g with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pesto pasta to small portions (≤150 g) and request homemade pesto with reduced parmesan and no added salt, as the sodium content of 329 mg/100 g approaches the threshold for borderline-high sodium density and parmesan cheese is a concentrated sodium source.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat pasta base with moderate glycemic load despite low GI (49)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of white pasta is relatively low (≈49), a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 41 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~20, which is moderate-to-high; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wholegrain or legume-based pasta, limit portions to 150–180 g cooked, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (10.5 g/100 g) and sodium (329 mg/100 g); partially offset by EVOO-derived monounsaturated fats and polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pesto pasta contains 10.5 g fat/100 g, of which approximately 2.2 g is saturated fat; the extra virgin olive oil base provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and oleocanthal, but the sodium content (329 mg/100 g) and caloric density (184 kcal/100 g) warrant portion control to ≤200 g per meal for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components from extra virgin olive oil (oleocanthal), basil, and pine nuts
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The extra virgin olive oil in pesto provides oleocanthal, a natural COX inhibitor with ibuprofen-like anti-inflammatory properties, and basil and pine nuts contribute polyphenols and vitamin E; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer homemade or EVOO-based pesto and substitute wholegrain pasta to reduce the refined carbohydrate inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pesto pasta is a classic Italian dish originating from the Ligurian region of Italy, consisting of cooked wheat pasta (typically spaghetti, linguine, or penne) tossed with basil pesto sauce. Traditional pesto is made by blending fresh basil leaves, extra virgin olive oil, pine nuts, parmesan cheese, and garlic into a smooth, vibrant green sauce. The dish has a rich, herbaceous, and slightly nutty flavour with a silky, coating texture. In Singapore and other Western-influenced food environments, pesto pasta is commonly served as a working lunch or casual dinner option. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides 184 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 16.5 g carbohydrate. The fat content is predominantly unsaturated, derived from extra virgin olive oil and pine nuts, which contribute cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols including oleocanthal. Sodium is moderately elevated at 329 mg/100 g due to parmesan cheese and any added salt in commercial pesto. Dietary fibre is low at 1.2 g/100 g, reflecting the refined wheat pasta base. The glycemic index of white pasta is approximately 49 (low), but the glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is approximately 20 (moderate). Clinically, pesto pasta is borderline-unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or type 2 diabetes without portion modification, but the EVOO component provides anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Substituting wholegrain or legume-based pasta significantly improves the fibre content and glycemic profile.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060095) with a reported confidence of 0.95. GI value of 49 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for white pasta (boiled). Saturated fat estimate of 2.2 g/100 g is derived from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable pesto pasta compositions and is consistent with the total fat value reported by HPB.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite pesto pasta dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (F05060095) as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible/absent for EVOO and pasta-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). Saturated fat of 2.2 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference data for pesto pasta (closest approved-source equivalent) and is consistent with the total fat value of 10.5 g/100 g reported by HPB. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; pesto sauce does not materially alter the pasta GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 250 g standard serving: (49 × 41.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 20.2. This is a Western/Italian dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, etc.) are not applicable and are correctly set to null. The dish is not halal-certified by default due to parmesan cheese (animal rennet) and the absence of halal certification for commercial pesto products.
